full_transcript.text: decomposing it, breaking it down to all the line items, understanding what your schedule drivers are and then resourcing them and addressing areas where we're stuck or need to make a decision, that's, that's how you pull it in.

full_transcript.speaker: Ti Morse
full_transcript.text: I think we like to prepare for the last disaster. And so if you see some like major earthquake or a fire burn down your house suddenly everyone wants to buy earthquake insurance or like fire insurance. I I saw Elon tweet a while back where he's like war with China is basically virtually guaranteed it's just a question of when. Um and like Anduril has this internal policy where they want, you know, most done by like 2027 or something like this. In a very like unfortunate scenario where we were to go to war with China even earlier than 2028, how does that impact like our ability to produce energy?

full_transcript.speaker: Scott Nolan
full_transcript.text: Um, in the nuclear supply chain it it doesn't, uh, fortunately. So, right now all of our enriched, you know, if we can talk about the different steps in the supply chain but from mining to conversion into gas to enrichment to deconversion and then fuel fabrication, these five steps currently in the US don't don't involve China. So, mining is in the US in places like Wyoming and Texas, uh but also Canada, Australia, uh other places in in Europe.

full_transcript.speaker: Ti Morse
full_transcript.text: Like Kazakhstan?

full_transcript.speaker: Scott Nolan
full_transcript.text: Yep. So uranium comes from a bunch of sources that are not China. Uh, conversion today is done in the US and Canada and also in Europe and most US utilities purchase from US and Canada. And then, uh enrichment is a European, uh and Russia supplied step of the supply chain. And then finally fuel fabrication is primarily done in the US by a mix of US and European companies. And so in nuclear, there's there really is not much impact other other sources of energy there would be.

full_transcript.speaker: Ti Morse
full_transcript.text: Like solar panels.

full_transcript.speaker: Scott Nolan
full_transcript.text: Like, yeah, like solar, yeah probably, range, batteries, a range of things.

full_transcript.speaker: Scott Nolan
full_transcript.text: You know, if we rewind like five years ago, China was a very, very tiny part of global enrichment capacity. If you look today they're roughly 15% and next decade if if they keep growing they will be 30%. So to your question of, you know, conflict with China or China's role in in the nuclear supply chain it's growing extremely rapidly, um to where yeah they could be neck and neck with Russia and China next decade if the US does nothing. But the same thing was true of of launch if if we're going back to the SpaceX story again where 2002 US was below 30% I think of global launch capacity and now is over 90% of global launch capacity with China as number two. And if the US had stayed where it was China would be number one. So I think on this question of like the US's role and China's role in this industry, the US has primarily just stayed flat for decades, China's growing rapidly. This is not the only sector where that's true, but it's it's one of these situations where if the US doesn't do something...

full_transcript.speaker: Ti Morse
full_transcript.text: And bring back, you know, bring back its own capability and expand that capability rapidly, China will dictate where the industry goes. And so, you know, that's certainly part of our mission is to bring back the US capability, but not just for an onshoring thing, but for a move the whole industry forward thing. And so it's, yeah, it's not just, is the US capable, can we supply our own, you know, power plants, but can we do that in a way that's so low cost that advanced reactors become much, much more viable and we get them everywhere and we get even safer baseload power, even cleaner baseload power at much lower cost? And so, you know, you look at the stats and nuclear energy statistically has been the safest, cleanest form of baseload for a very long time by a wide margin, but it has not been the cheapest form. It's been more expensive than other forms. And so...

full_transcript.speaker: Scott Nolan
full_transcript.text: Is that mostly like a regulatory issue?

full_transcript.speaker: Ti Morse
full_transcript.text: I think it's a bunch of issues. A lot of people like to blame the regulator and say the regulator makes it too hard to do things. In our experience, that's not true at all. I think first industry I worked in, space launch, people would have said, 'Why is space launch so expensive?' and you'd say, 'Well, it's really hard and you know, you need to get this reliability and maybe the regulations are hard.' And then it just turns out that a lot of the organizations were just doing things the way that they'd always done them, that they were cost-plus organizations, that they didn't have a lot of incentive to actually push the limits, to actually innovate and question why they were doing things, question the requirements. And I think what we've seen now in space launch, for example, is it's even safer, even more reliable, even lower cost. And that's just through getting iterations. So, and by scaling up. So, yeah, our goal is not just bring back US capability, it's not just, you know, make it lower cost, it's also make it more scalable so we can power all the things the US wants to do.

full_transcript.speaker: Scott Nolan
full_transcript.text: What have been the biggest conventional wisdoms in turning on this sort of operation that were actually wrong when you looked at the facts?

full_transcript.speaker: Ti Morse
full_transcript.text: Maybe the most like, the least obvious thing on questioned requirements around a company like us or even some of the nuclear companies in general, reactor companies, people would think that the entire team must be comprised of people with nuclear PhDs. And then when you actually- and I think that's the natural intuition. You would say, 'Hey, this is a nuclear energy company, therefore it's a nuclear science company, therefore you need nuclear engineers.' And you do, and you need people who understand the licensing, safety, in the case of reactors, nuclear reactions, and so it's a part of the team, but it's not the whole team. And so now you look at some-

full_transcript.speaker: Scott Nolan
full_transcript.text: Nuclear reactor companies and yes they have a handful of nuclear engineers, but it's mostly mechanical, aerospace, electrical, software, building the other systems that go into it with only a core group that's really looking at the nuclear reactions and making sure that you're going to get the power production you think out of a reactor.

full_transcript.speaker: Scott Nolan
full_transcript.text: In our case, it's even one step further where, yes, there's nuclear engineers on our team and they're a really important part for making sure that our system's safe and that there are not nuclear reactions. But you look at an enrichment facility and there's actually no nuclear reactions occurring, there's no chemical reactions occurring, it's simply phase change and separation. And so the team that you need to do that, again, yes, we'll have some nuclear engineers, we'll have some chemical engineers, not for the sake of chemical reactions, but like process development, making sure you're getting the performance of the system that you need, making sure, you know, your pumps, power systems, everything are what you think they should be, what you think they're going to do. But it's mostly software, electrical, mechanical, aerospace engineers, and which is why we're, you know, we chose to locate the company in Los Angeles, really the hub for that. So your number one assumption would be, oh, we need a lot of nuclear engineers. We need to be located in or at a national lab or right next door to one in a historically nuclear community. And after thinking about that the very beginning of the company and, okay, should we locate the company in Oak Ridge? There's an incredible amount of talent there in the nuclear industry. Should we locate it somewhere else, Los Alamos? We realized, no, we need to locate the company where most of the employees are going to be coming from and where we think we can recruit great nuclear engineers to, which is L.A.

full_transcript.speaker: Ti Morse
full_transcript.text: Yeah, this is similar to what Elon has kind of done where Dwarkesh was asking about like, do you try to like optimize for PhDs or something? And he was like, no, I just want great engineers. And great engineers don't always have PhDs.

full_transcript.speaker: Scott Nolan
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I mean, great engineers even might not even have a master's degree or even an undergrad degree. Um, there's people who are incredible technicians who are basically like very hands-on engineers who might not have gone to college, but maybe they have worked on like, you know, all sorts of mechanical systems for decades, even from when they were a kid because they were, you know, tuning up their shifter cart or like doing car engine rebuilds or they were working on hot rods. And so I've worked with people before who are some of the best engineers who don't have a formal degree at all, but have really built a lot of systems and have really smart ways of doing things that someone who maybe spent four years in university wouldn't realize.

full_transcript.speaker: Ti Morse
full_transcript.text: They don't have a traditional proof of work, but they have like real real world stuff.

full_transcript.speaker: Scott Nolan
full_transcript.text: Yep.

full_transcript.speaker: Ti Morse
full_transcript.text: I think one thing that's very important to cover is basically the idea that we've got this massive tsunami, which is AI, and you have to turn on all these data centers. And I think a lot of data centers, you need nuclear power.

full_transcript.speaker: Scott Nolan
full_transcript.text: If you can't actually produce power because you don't have enriched uranium, it doesn't work.

full_transcript.speaker: Ti Morse
full_transcript.text: Yeah, when we started the company, the whole AI data center wave was not, not really top of mind for people or even for us. We were just very focused on bringing back capability as quickly as possible so there wasn't the AI data center boom, there wasn't the Russia ban. It was we need to do this and we need to do it for the advanced reactors as quickly as we can so that they can succeed. Now it's become obvious that not only do we need to do that, but we need to massively increase the amount of nuclear fuel produced to power all the reactors that people are working on, that the hyperscalers are planning to put at their sites behind the meter. And so it's one of the most urgent things we think about is how do we build for capacity? So right now as we build enrichment capacity in Kentucky, we're sizing that to completely satisfy the domestic industry all the way through 2040. We want to get way ahead of it and make sure there's more than enough capacity to power this like first in decades net meaningful expansion of the US grid. So, I mean the way we think about it is not just cost, it's not just schedule, it's how do we do these things at scale and allow ourselves to scale up capacity both responsively as we see the demand coming so on short timelines and then in a way that really moves the needle. And so yeah that's something we think about constantly.

full_transcript.speaker: Scott Nolan
full_transcript.text: How did that change the way that you think about building the company and your need to go produce way more uranium?

full_transcript.speaker: Ti Morse
full_transcript.text: The AI boom makes it even more obvious how quickly we need to expand grid capacity. So if we go back to something I said earlier, it's 2010, the US and China were exactly at the same place on the grid. Today they've doubled it. So in about 15 years it is the case that if you really want to you can double your grid from where we're sitting today. And then if you look at the AI demand it's, you know, it's dramatic. It's like by 2030, I believe the AI, the latest projections are that the AI data center demand for electricity is going to completely consume or equal today's grid.

full_transcript.speaker: Scott Nolan
full_transcript.text: Eclipse, yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Ti Morse
full_transcript.text: And so clearly we do need to double. Can we double by 2030? I think that's a big feat, but what all the data centers are planning is, okay, bringing new transmission online that quickly and going for centralized nuclear plants is going to be too hard. We're going to go behind the meter. And so they're going to bring their own power, they're going to be deploying SMRs. I think we see that really ramp in the 2030s. It'll take a couple of years before they're through licensing and really scaling, but we're planning for ramping in the 2030s and so as we think ahead to how are we doing things, everything that we're doing is designed to scale. So how do we unlock things like mass manufacturing? All the progress in the last really 30 plus years since anyone has completely re-architected enrichment. How do we leverage all those things along with the supply chains for

full_transcript.speaker: Scott Nolan
full_transcript.text: them to unlock rapid scaling next decade once we're online.

full_transcript.speaker: Scott Nolan
full_transcript.text: Do you have any really great stories from working at SpaceX that kind of shifted the way that you think?

full_transcript.speaker: Ti Morse
full_transcript.text: Um, you know, to your question on schedule. And how do you drive schedule? The dimensions I mentioned were, you know, put things in parallel that can be put in parallel, question your granularity on timelines, make decisions without belaboring them, like once you're at a pretty good level of confidence, move forward.

full_transcript.speaker: Ti Morse
full_transcript.text: I think the fourth dimension that may be very hard for people to do sometimes is don't beat your head against the wall. If you're working on something and it's just a huge roadblock, to the question of like, make a decision when you're pretty confident, also don't be afraid to go back and make a different decision.

full_transcript.speaker: Ti Morse
full_transcript.text: And so early days when I was there of, you know, working on the Merlin engine system alongside some incredible people, just as a, you know, a kid out of school, a couple years out of school, I was working on a lot of the structural thermal analysis and design around those engine systems.

full_transcript.speaker: Ti Morse
full_transcript.text: And I remember one night, you know, at the time we had an ablative chamber. And an ablative chamber is like basically a composite wrapped chamber that burns away as you operate the rocket, as you operate the engine.

full_transcript.speaker: Ti Morse
full_transcript.text: And so this ablative chamber was having a few issues. Like, we were operating it probably higher pressures and temperatures than most ablatives had been run previously. They'd been proven like extremely reliable and low cost before, but we were running into these persistent issues with them.

full_transcript.speaker: Ti Morse
full_transcript.text: And late one night, a few people, you know, had gotten together to talk about this and I remember the question came up like, what if, what if we just switch? Like, what if this is a fool's errand to get this ablative working at the thrust levels we need, at the pressures and temperatures we need?

full_transcript.speaker: Ti Morse
full_transcript.text: What if we just bite the bullet and go as fast as we can rethinking this, accepting that maybe this was not the right path and going to a regeneratively cooled all metal engine? And that's what we did.

full_transcript.speaker: Ti Morse
full_transcript.text: And, um, you know, now obviously the Merlin engine and Raptor engines work this way and none that SpaceX flies are ablative engines. And so one lesson I learned was, yeah, try and move fast, try and make the right decisions.

full_transcript.speaker: Ti Morse
full_transcript.text: Don't make irreversible decisions, but also don't get completely stuck. And if you're just going down a path that doesn't feel like it's going anywhere, don't have ego around it. Like, even enforce a meritocracy in your own mind and just go back.

full_transcript.speaker: Ti Morse
full_transcript.text: And yes, there's going to be some moving targets. Yes, things are going to change. There might be additional work to do that you're hoping to not do. Maybe you've got to take a hit on, on, you know, spend some more money to go fix this, but don't get too hung up on things.

full_transcript.speaker: Ti Morse
full_transcript.text: And don't have any like sort of sacred ideas that you cannot question, like question everything.

full_transcript.speaker: Scott Nolan
full_transcript.text: Question everything, question requirements, question the path, and just try and find the best path forward at all times, uh while obviously like trying to somewhat minimize chaos. And so, yeah, you know, this almost gets back to the the founder question that you asked before. Um, inside a company when there's big changes and big shifts in direction in response like new information, it's gonna mean people have to do more work and ideally that wouldn't be the case but, um, sometimes the right path is is to to change and and adjust and I think, uh, yeah, it can it can take someone with like who who really has the big picture in mind, um, whether that's a founder who, uh, might be the CEO or a founder who's not the CEO, um, really saying, 'Hey, let's let's think more about why we're doing this. We've all been going down this path together but it's okay to to change direction' and, um, that that was a lesson that I wouldn't have expected to learn, especially given, you know, SpaceX being so known for just the classic things: fast schedule, hard charging, cost, um, but sometimes you have to actually go back and and change the way you're doing stuff. So.

full_transcript.speaker: Ti Morse
full_transcript.text: Another thing that I've heard consistently is that Elon will make decisions, like, think through a problem and make decisions that no one else kind of sees and then it seems like a really hard, like, the wrong decision short term and then later on, maybe even a year or two later, it becomes the right decision. Was there any moment at SpaceX where you saw Elon make a call that everyone else thought was wrong that ended up being right?

full_transcript.speaker: Scott Nolan
full_transcript.text: I mean the classic Tesla example is Lidar versus no Lidar. And I think for a long time it the the thinking was, 'Hey, a Lidar is only a few thousand dollars, maybe it's tens of thousands, but why don't we just put it on the car, like, make life easy on ourselves?' And then years later you realize, 'Hey, it's not a perception problem, we can perceive the environment with cameras, it's a controls problem, it's a logic problem.' Um, so that's that's probably the most salient example where even I circa 2010 would have said, 'Why not use Lidars? They're they're here, we have them. Um, yes they're expensive, but the cost will go down' and then it's like, 'Okay, but how much will the cost really go down? Why don't we just think through this 10 years out and it's gonna be cameras.' At SpaceX, you know, I think I think that thing might have been going to Falcon 9 sooner than people would have expected. What I was there we were at at some points were working on both Falcon 1 and Falcon 9. And Falcon 1 was still in the process of of having its first successful flight and yet we were working on Falcon 9. And that was okay, you know, uh, from an from a resourcing standpoint because many of us, you know, we'd worked on the Merlin engine, Merlin engine was working, it was it was working pretty well and we knew it'd be used on Falcon 9, so that that product could be reused. But there was a lot more rocket to develop and I think, you know, at one point Falcon 9 was Falcon 5 and then it was realized, 'Hey, for this thing to really cover the market and to give...'

full_transcript.speaker: Scott Nolan
full_transcript.text: the performance we need, it needs to be Falcon 9. And so yeah, I would say there were probably people in the company who were surprised by that like, oh wow, we were doing Falcon 5, now we're already doing Falcon 9. Is this the right choice? And obviously it was.

full_transcript.speaker: Scott Nolan
full_transcript.text: And so I think it can be things as simple as that of when do you launch your next product line, when do you when do you take on another challenge, when do you work on something that's like much sooner than you would have thought while you're still trying to get the first thing successful.

full_transcript.speaker: Scott Nolan
full_transcript.text: But clearly proven correct, clearly Falcon 1 was serving what turned out to be a very small market for a very long time and that market didn't emerge very quickly and the correct thing was to jump straight to the larger satellite market.

full_transcript.speaker: Scott Nolan
full_transcript.text: And I think there were other examples of this too, even on the tech side, really that switch from ablative rocket nozzle to regen, you were just adding a whole block in the schedule for its development versus hoping to just get there and then that's turned out to be absolutely correct versus the unreliability that would have come through the ablative engine.

full_transcript.speaker: Scott Nolan
full_transcript.text: And there were other decisions like that too like, hey instead of this type of nozzle for the vacuum engine we could use this other more exotic but really exciting other one and the decision was, no, don't worry about that. We need we just need to get this working and don't worry about that potentially higher performance thing. This one is known to be reliable, we can buy it, it's cheap enough and it's going to get us to where we need to go so let's do that.

full_transcript.speaker: Scott Nolan
full_transcript.text: And it was just many, many practical decisions like that along the way. I would say like many more practical decisions that were obvious when you looked at what the long-term mission was versus really, really deep tech breakthroughs and I think seeing SpaceX succeed over the years it's been evident that it's just make a lot of decisions that are in line with the core metric that you're going after and um, you know, if you if you stay focused on that you can do pretty well.

full_transcript.speaker: Scott Nolan
full_transcript.text: Even to that point, on day one of joining SpaceX we had an employee handbook and I remember the first page of the employee handbook I think in big bold letters said this is not a science experiment, this is an engineering problem. And so yeah, to that question of like what unconventional choices were made that turned out to be right, I think they either followed two categories.

full_transcript.speaker: Scott Nolan
full_transcript.text: One was those choices were pragmatic and very focused on cost and schedule. Or category two, those choices were they really looked at the big picture and let's not optimize for a dead end that's going to come two years from now like in the case of lidar. Let's think a decade out. What are the trends? What things can we really bet on that we know are going to be true? And yeah, how do we just optimize for that?

full_transcript.speaker: Ti Morse
full_transcript.text: While you're mainly focused on Founders Fund, what was the biggest thing that you kind of evolved in your own way of company building and your thinking on company?

full_transcript.speaker: Ti Morse
full_transcript.text: building during that like huge 10 plus year stint.

full_transcript.speaker: Scott Nolan
full_transcript.text: Yeah, when I started at Founders Fund, you know, this was 2011. There were a lot of at the time, you know, Founders Fund was really focused on two things: one, back founders unconditionally, and two, what things can we invest in that are great that otherwise won't receive funding. So where can we make a big impact both on the team side and then market or sector side or technology side. And so there was this belief that technology was what could fuel the next wave of growth in the US, and it was systematically underfunding, and that we should be trying to to help that succeed, you know, through capital. When I started in 2011, the way that that showed up was you would, you know, there were a lot of very early-stage engineering-driven companies, hard tech, like engineering-driven companies were not nearly as popular as they are today. The focus was really things like software and consumer. And so

full_transcript.speaker: Scott Nolan
full_transcript.text: early on for the first few years, I was I was much more focused on, I'd say, early-stage, smaller checks, seed-stage checks into engineering-driven companies, which did remain my focus over the over the 12 plus years. But over time, realize more and more some of these companies work at a much larger scale than you ever think that they will, and the correct thing is actually to focus on the very small handful of companies that are going to be the most successful, that are going to be the most important, and where you really feel the most conviction in the team, and just work with those companies. And yes, that means you're not getting to invest in the seed-stage company at necessarily a really, you know, attractive valuation. Maybe you're accepting, hey, I'm not investing in a company that's 10 million pre, I'm investing in a company that's a billion valuation, but what are the odds that this goes 100x? I think these things are sometimes scale-invariant where the probability of going from 10 employees to 100 employees to 1,000 employees, you might be more likely to go from 100 to 1,000 than from 10 to 100. And so, like, looked at in a different way, the conclusion I have is that sometimes it's easier to build a really hard company than it is to build an easy company. At the end of the day, it gets back to like what really important problem are you solving that's not getting solved, and I think if you if you are focused on that, if your mission as a company is doing something that's really critical, potentially really challenging, which is why it hasn't been done to date, you can just attract the best people, you can usually get the resources that you need to go do this if you're executing well, and in total, it actually becomes a lot easier than trying to start like a small business or something that um is much more competed over. And so I think one way that people understand this is

full_transcript.speaker: Scott Nolan
full_transcript.text: ...start companies in areas with- without much competition, avoid competition, etc. I think the more meaningful way to think about that is, don't focus on problems that are already getting solved or adjacent to problems currently being solved that you know are going to be tackled by someone else. Focus on the problems that are big and that are just not getting solved. Dedicate yourself to them. If you do that, it will be very challenging, but it may actually turn out to be easier than starting the next small business or the next small software company.

full_transcript.speaker: Ti Morse
full_transcript.text: Yeah, it's funny because you can kind of attract a different types of talent. Like the very best talent in- from what I've seen and experienced, the very best talent wants to work on the hardest problems, even if it like- basically gives them less money or these other things. Like the enjoyment of working on something that's important and valuable is- is more attractive to them.

full_transcript.speaker: Scott Nolan
full_transcript.text: Yeah, maybe the problem's twice as hard, but you get ten times better people. And so net, it's somehow easier.

highlights: The U.S. faces a significant energy security challenge due to a 2028 ban on Russian uranium, which currently provides 25% of the nation's supply, necessitating a rapid restoration of domestic enrichment capabilities.

Adopting an "engineering problem" mindset rather than a "science experiment" approach is essential for scaling deep tech, prioritizing aggressive parallelization and rapid decision-making to drive down costs.
Projected AI data center energy demands could eclipse the current U.S. power grid by 2030, making clean, reliable nuclear baseload power a critical requirement for future economic growth.

It is often easier to build a company addressing a massive, neglected problem than a conventional startup because the sheer importance of the mission attracts exponentially better talent and resources.

Nuclear energy's primary bottleneck is a lack of industrial iteration; reducing costs requires re-architecting enrichment facilities into scalable, factory-built models rather than treating them as bespoke construction projects.
summary:
This transcript features Scott Nolan, CEO of General Matter, discussing the critical need to restore domestic uranium enrichment capacity in the United States. Nolan explains that the U.S. currently relies on Russia for approximately 25% of its enriched uranium, a dependency that poses a severe risk given a bipartisan import ban set to take full effect in 2028. This supply vulnerability is exacerbated by the "AI boom," which is projected to double energy demands, and the rapid expansion of China’s electrical grid. Nolan argues that nuclear power is the only clean, economical baseload energy capable of meeting this demand, yet the U.S. currently lacks the domestic infrastructure to produce the advanced fuels required for next-generation reactors.

To address this, Nolan founded General Matter to treat uranium enrichment as a scalable engineering problem rather than a "science experiment." Drawing on his early experience at SpaceX, he emphasizes a culture of "maniacal urgency," utilizing parallel scheduling, first-principles thinking, and a focus on reducing "separative work unit" (SWU) costs. By locating their first facility in Paducah, Kentucky—a community with a historical legacy of nuclear support—and recruiting top-tier engineering talent from the aerospace and tech sectors, the company aims to provide a reliable domestic backstop for the U.S. energy grid by the end of the decade. 

The overall theme of the conversation is the strategic necessity of reshoring industrial capabilities to ensure national security and economic growth. Nolan posits that the U.S. undervalued its supply chain during the era of global free trade, allowing adversaries like Russia and China to dominate the nuclear sector. He concludes that by applying high-speed startup methodologies to hard industrial problems, the U.S. can regain its global influence and build a scalable, clean energy future that can power everything from AI data centers to advanced defense systems.
